The left ventricle does not receive deoxygenated blood from the whole body. It receives oxygenated blood from the left atrium and is responsible for pumping the oxygenated blood to the rest of the body.

The aorta is not directly involved in receiving deoxygenated blood from the whole body. It is the largest artery in the body and carries oxygenated blood from the left ventricle to systemic circulation, supplying oxygen and nutrients to all the organs and tissues.

questions.text: In pressure flow theory, when water moves out of sieve tube cells by osmosis, it lowers the _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Solute potential refers to the effect of solute concentration on the movement of water. It is a measure of the tendency of water to move from a region of higher water potential (lower solute concentration) to a region of lower water potential (higher solute concentration). In the pressure flow theory, water moves out of sieve tube cells due to a decrease in solute concentration, which increases the solute potential.

In the pressure flow theory of phloem transport, the movement of sugars from source (e.g. leaves) to sink (e.g. roots) requires a pressure gradient. This gradient is established when sugars are actively transported into the sieve tube cells at the source, causing an increase in hydrostatic pressure. Water then moves into the sieve tube cells by osmosis, increasing hydrostatic pressure. This high pressure at the source end of the phloem pushes the sugars and water solution towards the sink. At the sink, sugars are removed from the phloem, causing hydrostatic pressure to decrease, allowing water to move out of the sieve tube cells and back into the xylem.

Water potential is a measure of the potential energy of water in a system and is influenced by various factors such as solute concentration, pressure, and gravity. In the pressure flow theory, when water moves out of sieve tube cells, it decreases the water potential within those cells. This decrease in water potential creates a pressure gradient that facilitates the flow of water and nutrients.

Source concentration refers to the concentration of solutes (such as sugars) at the source, where they are actively loaded into the sieve tube cells. In the pressure flow theory, solutes are loaded into sieve tube cells at the source and create a higher solute concentration. This high concentration of solutes drives water to move into the sieve tube cells by osmosis, which eventually leads to a decrease in solute concentration.

The rate of transpiration refers to the rate at which water vapor is lost from the leaves of a plant through stomata. While transpiration is an important process in plant water movement, it is not directly related to the pressure flow theory and the movement of water out of sieve tube cells. The pressure flow theory focuses on the movement of water and solutes within the phloem tissue.

questions.explanation: In the pressure flow theory of phloem transport, the movement of sugars from source (e.g. leaves) to sink (e.g. roots) requires a pressure gradient. This gradient is established when sugars are actively transported into the sieve tube cells at the source, causing an increase in hydrostatic pressure. Water then moves into the sieve tube cells by osmosis, increasing hydrostatic pressure. This high pressure at the source end of the phloem pushes the sugars and water solution towards the sink. At the sink, sugars are removed from the phloem, causing hydrostatic pressure to decrease, allowing water to move out of the sieve tube cells and back into the xylem.

questions.text: Evaporation of water from the aerial parts of the plants, especially through the stomata of the leave creates a force which is cause of water movement in higher plant is _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Root pressure refers to the upward force exerted by the roots of a plant, resulting in the movement of water and solutes into the xylem vessels. Root pressure is typically involved in the movement of water at a relatively low height and is not the primary force responsible for long-distance water transport in higher plants.

There is no specific phenomenon known as leaf pressure related to water movement in higher plants. The main forces involved in water movement are typically associated with root pressure, transpiration pull, and capillary action

Imbibition is the process by which substances, such as water, are absorbed and held by the surface of solid materials, such as cell walls or fibers. It plays a role in the initial uptake of water by plant cells, but it is not the primary force responsible for long-distance water movement in higher plants.

Transpiration is the process by which water vapour is lost from the aerial parts of a plant, primarily through the stomata on the leaves. It is a significant factor in the movement of water through the plant. Transpiration creates a negative pressure gradient that pulls water from the roots through the xylem vessels.

When water evaporates from the aerial parts of the plant, it creates a negative pressure or tension in the xylem vessels, which is known as transpiration pull. This negative pressure generates a force that pulls the water up through the roots and stems towards the leaves, which is called the ascent of sap.

questions.explanation: When water evaporates from the aerial parts of the plant, it creates a negative pressure or tension in the xylem vessels, which is known as transpiration pull. This negative pressure generates a force that pulls the water up through the roots and stems towards the leaves, which is called the ascent of sap.

questions.text: Which of the following types of transpiration account for 5 - 7 % of total transpiration that take place in plants?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cuticular transpiration is the loss of water vapor through the cuticle, a waxy, water-repelling layer covering the surface of leaves, stems, and other plant organs. This type of transpiration accounts for 5-7% of the total transpiration in plants.

Lenticular transpiration occurs through lenticels, which are small openings or pores on the surface of stems and woody organs. Lenticels allow for gas exchange and can also contribute to water loss. However, lenticular transpiration typically accounts for a smaller portion of total transpiration compared to other types. This option is not correct.

Stomatal transpiration is the most significant form of transpiration in plants. It occurs through the stomata, which are small openings primarily found on the surface of leaves. Stomata regulate the exchange of gases and water vapor between the plant and the environment. Stomatal transpiration is the primary mechanism responsible for water loss in plants. This option is not correct.

This option seems to be incorrectly worded, as leaves are not a type of transpiration. Transpiration occurs through specific mechanisms (such as stomata or cuticle) present on leaves rather than the leaves themselves. This option is not correct.

Stem transpiration refers to water loss that occurs through the surface of stems. While stems can contribute to overall transpiration, they typically have a lesser impact compared to leaves, which have a larger surface area and more stomata. This option is not correct.

questions.explanation: Cuticular transpiration is the loss of water vapour through the cuticle, a waxy, water-repelling layer covering the surface of leaves, stems, and other plant organs. This type of transpiration accounts for 5-7% of the total transpiration in plants.

options.explanation: Option A is the hypertonic solution, as it causes the cells to shrink due to the movement of water out of the cell towards the higher solute concentration. Option B is the isotonic solution, and option C is the hypotonic solution as it causes the cells to fill with water due to the movement of water towards the lower solute concentration. questions.text: Blood containing CO2 is:

options.explanation: Oxygenated blood is bright red because of the presence of oxygen. Oxygen binds to hemoglobin, a protein in red blood cells, and gives the blood its red color.

Deoxygenated blood is darker red or even blue because it has less oxygen. When oxygen leaves the blood, carbon dioxide binds to hemoglobin instead. Carbon dioxide makes the blood appear darker because it absorbs more light.

Blood containing carbon dioxide (CO2) is reddish purple in color. This is because CO2 binds to hemoglobin, a protein in red blood cells, and changes its shape. The change in shape makes the blood appear darker and more purple. Blood that is high in oxygen is bright red. As blood travels through the body and delivers oxygen to the tissues, it picks up CO2 as a waste product. The more CO2 that is in the blood, the darker it will appear.

questions.text: Transport of glucose across the cell membrane occurs by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Simple diffusion is a type of passive transport that does not require protein carriers. Glucose can diffuse across the cell membrane by simple diffusion if the concentration of glucose is higher outside the cell than inside the cell.

The transport of glucose across the cell membrane occurs by facilitated diffusion. Facilitated diffusion is a type of passive transport that uses protein carriers to help glucose move across the cell membrane. Glucose is a polar molecule, which means that it is attracted to water. The cell membrane is made up of a lipid bilayer, which is hydrophobic, or repellant to water. This means that glucose cannot easily diffuse across the cell membrane on its own. Facilitated diffusion helps glucose to overcome this barrier by providing a pathway for it to move through the membrane.

Osmosis is the movement of water across a semipermeable membrane. If the concentration of water is higher outside the cell than inside the cell, water will move into the cell by osmosis. This will create a concentration gradient, which will drive the movement of glucose into the cell by facilitated diffusion.

Primary active transport is a type of active transport that uses energy to move molecules across the cell membrane against their concentration gradient. Glucose can be transported by primary active transport in some cells, but this is not the main way that glucose moves into cells.

Secondary active transport is a type of active transport that uses the energy from the movement of one molecule to move another molecule across the cell membrane. Glucose can be transported by secondary active transport in some cells, but this is not the main way that glucose moves into cells.

questions.explanation: Glucose being neutral can pass through cell membranes easily by facilitated diffusion.

questions.text: Cardiac output is increased by all of the following EXCEPT:

questions.text: When the concentration of the external medium is equal to the concentration of the internal medium of a cell, the situation is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In a hypertonic solution, the concentration of solutes is higher outside the cell compared to the inside. This creates a concentration gradient, causing water to move out of the cell, leading to cell shrinkage or plasmolysis.

In a hypotonic solution, the concentration of solutes is lower outside the cell compared to the inside. This creates a concentration gradient, causing water to move into the cell, leading to cell swelling or even bursting.

When the concentration of the external medium is equal to the concentration of the internal medium of a cell, the situation is called isotonic. In an isotonic solution, the concentration of solutes (ions and molecules) is the same inside and outside the cell. As a result, there is no net movement of water across the cell membrane, and the cell maintains its shape and volume.

questions.text: Movement of the material across the cell membrane which does not require expenditure of metabolic energy is called:

options.explanation: Active transport is a biological process that moves ions or molecules against their concentration gradient, from an area of lower concentration to an area of higher concentration. This process requires the expenditure of energy, typically derived from the hydrolysis of ATP (adenosine triphosphate). Active transport is essential for maintaining proper concentrations of ions and molecules within cells and across cell membranes. Examples include the sodium-potassium pump in animal cells and proton pumps in plant cells, both vital for cellular functions and maintaining cell homeostasis.

Passive transport is a biological process that moves ions or molecules across a cell membrane without the use of energy. It occurs along the concentration gradient, from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration. There are two main types of passive transport: Simple Diffusion: Small, non-polar molecules move directly through the lipid bilayer of the membrane. Facilitated Diffusion:Larger or polar molecules move through specific protein channels or carriers embedded in the membrane. Passive transport plays a crucial role in maintaining cell homeostasis by allowing essential substances to move into or out of cells without the expenditure of energy.

questions.text: A person got an infection, he became ill but then he survived. What do you think which of immunity he would have developed?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: 'Active immunity' is when the body itself gets exposed to the antigen to generate an immune response, whereby memory cells are produced which confer long lasting or lifelong immunity against the particular agent. This response may take some time to develop.
In 'artificial active immunity', the immunity is produced by intentionally exposing the individual to antigens in a vaccine (hence the term artificial, since exposure did not occur naturally).

Passive immunity occurs when an individual receives antibodies or immune components produced by another organism rather than by their own immune system. There are two main types: Natural Passive Immunity:This occurs when antibodies are transferred from mother to baby during pregnancy or through breast milk. The newborn benefits from the mother's antibodies for a temporary period, providing protection against certain infections. Artificial Passive Immunity:This involves the administration of pre-formed antibodies obtained from another individual or animal. This can provide immediate but temporary protection. For example, injecting someone with antibodies against a specific pathogen can offer rapid defense in situations like exposure to a disease or venom.

Naturally induced active immunity occurs when the body's immune system responds to an actual infection by pathogens or exposure to antigens in the environment. In this process, the immune system recognizes the invading pathogens, such as bacteria or viruses, and mounts a specific and targeted immune response. During the course of the infection, the immune system produces antibodies and memory cells. Antibodies help in the elimination of the pathogens, while memory cells "remember" the specific antigens, providing the basis for a faster and more robust immune response upon subsequent exposure to the same pathogen. This form of immunity is a natural consequence of the body's encounters with various infectious agents throughout life.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most widely accepted theory for the ascent of sap?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Water potential is somehow responsible for the ascent of sap, but it does not work because of gravitational pull. Hence, this option is incorrect.

The cohesion-tension theory says that due to the cohesion and adhesion of water molecules, water moves towards the aerial parts of the plant, and thus, it is the most widely accepted theory for the ascent of sap. Hence, this option is the correct one.

Root pressure theory says that sap ascent is due to root pressure, a positive pressure that develops in the xylem sap of the root of some plants. This is not a widely accepted theory as it is applicable only to a few plants. Hence, this option is incorrect.

The ambition theory says that water in plants rises up by ambition through thick walls of xylem cells and the sclerenchyma of the phloem. This theory is also not widely accepted; hence, the option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: There are many theories explaining the ascent of sap, including: 1. Cohesion-Tension Theory: The theory states that transpiration pull or tension, the cohesion property of water, and hydration of the cell walls (i.e., adhesion) are collectively responsible for the ascent of sap. 2. Root pressure theory: The theory states that the phenomenon of root pressure is responsible for the ascent of sap. 3. Imbibition: According to this theory, the water rises by imbibition through the thick walls of the xylem cells as well as those of the sclerenchyma of the phloem. The forces of imbibition range from 100 to 1000 atm, and this would seem adequate for carrying water to any required distance. Out of all these, the cohesion-tension theory is the most widely accepted.

questions.text: Cell-mediated immune response is given by:

options.explanation: Cell-mediated immunity is an immune response that does not involve antibodies. The humoral immune system deals with antigens from pathogens that are freely circulating, or outside the infected cells. questions.text: Pressure is highest in:

options.explanation: The aorta is the largest artery in the human body and serves as the main artery that carries oxygenated blood away from the heart to the rest of the body. While the aorta has relatively high pressure, the pressure decreases as blood moves through smaller arteries and into arterioles.

Arteries are vessels that carry blood away from the heart. They have thick, muscular walls that help maintain high pressure as blood is pumped by the heart. Arteries are known for their relatively high blood pressure compared to other parts of the circulatory system.

Capillaries are tiny, thin-walled blood vessels where the exchange of oxygen, nutrients, and waste products occurs between the blood and the surrounding tissues. Capillaries have low pressure to facilitate this exchange, and their walls are so thin that they allow for efficient diffusion.

Arterioles are small branches of arteries that lead to capillaries. While arterioles play a role in regulating blood flow to different parts of the body, their pressure is generally lower than that in larger arteries like the aorta. Pressure gradually decreases as blood moves from arteries to arterioles and eventually to capillaries.

questions.text: Pulse is found in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The pulse is typically detected in arteries. Arteries are blood vessels that carry oxygenated blood away from the heart to various parts of the body. The rhythmic expansion and relaxation of the arterial walls due to the surge of blood ejected by the heart with each heartbeat result in the palpable pulse.

Capillaries are the smallest blood vessels and are responsible for the exchange of oxygen, nutrients, and waste products between the blood and the surrounding tissues. questions.text: The loss of liquid via the hydathodes is called:

options.explanation: Imbibition is the process by which substances, especially water, are absorbed by and swell within the internal structure of a solid, such as seeds or cell walls. Guttation is the exudation of liquid, usually water, from plant leaves, typically at night, through specialized structures called hydathodes. It occurs due to root pressure and is a mechanism by which excess water and dissolved minerals are expelled from the plant.

Plasmolysis is the shrinking or contraction of the protoplast (cell contents) away from the cell wall in plant cells, often resulting from water loss and occurring in hypertonic conditions. It is not related to the loss of liquid via hydathodes.

questions.text: The total kinetic energy of water molecules is known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Water potential is a measure of the potential energy of water in a system and is a combination of pressure potential and osmotic potential. It is a fundamental concept in plant physiology and relates to the ability of water to move within plant tissues.

Pressure potential, also known as turgor pressure, is a component of water potential and represents the pressure exerted by the cell contents against the cell wall. It contributes to the overall water potential.

Osmotic potential is another component of water potential and is a measure of the influence of solute concentration on the movement of water. It is related to the tendency of water to move in response to solute gradients.

The total kinetic energy of water molecules is not typically referred to by any of these terms. Kinetic energy is a measure of the energy associated with the motion of water molecules but is not specifically encompassed by the terms listed.

These strips are formed in the cell walls of the endodermis and play a role in controlling the movement of water and solutes from the root cortex into the vascular tissue.

The cortex is a region of the root that lies between the epidermis and the vascular bundle. While it is a part of the root, Casparian strips are specifically associated with the endodermis within the cortex.

Vascular bundles are the central tissues in a plant stem or root responsible for the transport of water, nutrients, and other substances. Casparian strips are not directly associated with vascular bundles but rather with the endodermal cells that surround the vascular tissue.

questions.text: The attraction between water molecules and cell wall of xylem is termed as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cohesion refers to the tendency of molecules of the same kind to stick together. This phenomenon is particularly noticeable in liquids, where the molecules are in close proximity.Water molecules are attracted to each other due to hydrogen bonding. In a water molecule, the oxygen atom is more electronegative than the hydrogen atoms, creating a partial negative charge near the oxygen and partial positive charges near the hydrogens. This polarity allows water molecules to form hydrogen bonds with each other.
Surface tension is a measure of the cohesive forces at the surface of a liquid. It is the result of the tendency of liquid molecules to minimize their surface area.

Adhesion is the tendency of dissimilar particles or surfaces to cling to one another. It's the force of attraction that causes different substances to stick together. Adhesion is the opposite of cohesion, which refers to the tendency of like particles or surfaces to stick together.Water molecules exhibit adhesion when they "stick" to other surfaces, such as the walls of a tube or the surface of a solid. Adhesion, along with cohesion, contributes to capillary action, where liquids move against gravity in narrow spaces. It also influences the behavior of liquids in containers, such as meniscus formation in a graduated cylinder.

Imbibition is a process in which a substance absorbs water, causing it to increase in volume. This phenomenon is particularly observed in plant biology, where imbibition plays a crucial role in processes like seed germination and the movement of water through plant tissues.

questions.text: The external solution having more concentration than the cell sap is known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A hypertonic solution refers to an external solution that has a higher concentration of solutes compared to the cell sap or cytoplasm. When a cell is placed in a hypertonic solution, water tends to move out of the cell in an attempt to balance the concentration gradient. This can lead to cell shrinkage or plasmolysis.

A hypotonic solution on the other hand, has a lower concentration of solutes compared to the cell sap. When a cell is exposed to a hypotonic solution, water moves into the cell, causing it to swell or potentially burst.

An isotonic solution has the same concentration of solutes as the cell sap. In this case, there is no net movement of water into or out of the cell, resulting in the cell maintaining its normal shape and size.

questions.text: The beating of the heart in a human being is heard on the left side because:

options.text: Both the atria are towards the left side
options.explanation: This option suggests that the beating of the heart is heard on the left side because both the atria (the upper chambers of the heart) are positioned towards the left side. However, this is not the primary reason for the heartbeat being heard on the left side.

options.text: Both the ventricles are towards the left side
options.explanation: This option is the correct answer. It states that the beating of the heart is heard on the left side because both the ventricles (the lower chambers of the heart) are located towards the left side. The left ventricle, in particular, is responsible for pumping blood into the aorta, which is situated on the left side of the body. The sounds produced by the left ventricle's contraction and the closing of heart valves are more audible on the left side of the chest.

options.text: Entire heart is on the left side
options.explanation: This option suggests that the entire heart is positioned on the left side of the body. However, this is not accurate. While the heart is tilted slightly towards the left, it is not entirely located on the left side.

options.text: The aorta is on the left side
options.explanation: This option states that the beating of the heart is heard on the left side because the aorta, the largest blood vessel in the body, is situated on the left side. While the aorta does contribute to the audible sounds of the heartbeat, it is primarily the positioning of the ventricles that leads to the heartbeat being heard more prominently on the left side.

options.explanation: The pancreatic duct is a duct that runs through the pancreas and carries digestive enzymes from the pancreas to the small intestine. The digestive enzymes produced by the pancreas are essential for the digestion of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats, and they are released into the small intestine through the pancreatic duct. The pancreatic duct joins with the common bile duct, which carries bile from the liver and gallbladder to the small intestine, and together they empty into the duodenum, which is the first part of the small intestine. Therefore, the pancreatic duct plays an important role in the digestive system by transporting digestive enzymes from the pancreas to the small intestine.

The bile duct is a tube-like structure that carries bile from the liver and gallbladder to the small intestine. Bile is a greenish-yellow fluid that is produced by the liver and stored in the gallbladder. Bile contains bile salts, which are essential for the digestion and absorption of fats, and it also helps to neutralize stomach acid as it enters the small intestine. Therefore, the bile duct plays an important role in the digestive system by transporting bile from the liver and gallbladder to the small intestine.

The flow of lymph is always towards the thoracic duct. The thoracic duct is the largest lymphatic vessel in the body, and it is responsible for draining lymph from the lower body, the left side of the chest, and the left arm and neck. The thoracic duct empties into the left subclavian vein, which is located near the heart, and this is where lymph is returned to the bloodstream. Therefore, the flow of lymph is always towards the thoracic duct, which ultimately drains into the circulatory system near the heart.

The parotid gland is a large salivary gland that is located near the ear and extends down towards the angle of the jaw. The parotid gland secretes saliva into the mouth through the parotid duct, which opens into the inside of the cheek near the upper teeth. questions.text: Antigen is a foreign protein or any other molecule which stimulates the formation of :

questions.text: Skin and mucous membranes are part of the body defense system and they form the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a) Physical barrier: This option refers to the role of skin and mucous membranes in physically preventing the entry of pathogens and harmful substances into the body. Skin acts as a barrier that covers and protects the external surfaces of the body, while mucous membranes line various internal cavities and openings. Together, they create a physical barrier that helps prevent the entry of microbes.

b) Mechanical barriers: Mechanical barriers involve physical processes that contribute to defense. Skin provides a mechanical barrier through its multiple layers and the shedding of dead skin cells, which can help remove pathogens that may be present on the surface. Mucous membranes produce mucus, which traps foreign particles and microbes, preventing them from entering deeper tissues.

c) Chemical barriers: Skin and mucous membranes secrete various chemicals, such as enzymes and antimicrobial peptides, that can kill or inhibit the growth of pathogens. These chemicals act as chemical barriers that protect against infection. For example, the skin produces sebum, an oily substance that can inhibit the growth of certain bacteria.

d) Biological barriers: Biological barriers involve the action of immune cells and other components of the immune system to recognize and eliminate pathogens. While skin and mucous membranes play a role in defense, they are more accurately described as physical, mechanical, and chemical barriers rather than biological barriers.

questions.text: Snake bite is treated with which type of immunization?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a) Active immunization: Active immunization involves introducing a weakened or inactive form of the pathogen (antigen) into the body to stimulate the immune system to produce an immune response. This response results in the production of memory cells that can provide long-term protection against future infections. Examples of active immunization include vaccines, where a person receives a vaccine containing antigens from the pathogen to trigger an immune response.

b) Passive immunization: Passive immunization involves the direct transfer of pre-formed antibodies from an immune individual to a non-immune individual. This provides immediate but temporary protection against a specific disease. Passive immunization is often used for emergency situations, such as snake bites, where the individual receives antibodies (antivenom) from a donor source to neutralize the effects of venom.

c) Humoral immunity: Humoral immunity refers to the aspect of the immune response that involves the production of antibodies by B cells. Antibodies are proteins that can recognize and neutralize antigens. This type of immunity plays a crucial role in defending against infections caused by bacteria, viruses, and other extracellular pathogens.

d) Specific immunity: Specific immunity, also known as adaptive immunity, refers to the immune response that is tailored to recognize and target specific pathogens. This type of immunity develops over time as the immune system encounters different antigens. It includes both cell-mediated and humoral immune responses.

questions.explanation: Passive immunity provides immediate protection by injecting immunoglobulins, and this protection lasts for a short period of time, which is perfect for a snake bite.

options.explanation: Mature mammalian red blood cells, also known as erythrocytes, lack a nucleus. During their development in the bone marrow, erythrocytes have a nucleus, but they extrude it before entering the bloodstream. This allows them to have more space to carry the oxygen-carrying protein, hemoglobin. Without a nucleus, erythrocytes have a biconcave shape, which increases their surface area and allows for more efficient oxygen exchange. The lack of a nucleus also means that erythrocytes cannot divide or repair themselves, which is why their lifespan is typically around 120 days.

The tight junctions: The cells of the mucous membrane are held together by tight junctions, which are small gaps between the cells. questions.explanation: (a) : Stomata are the main organs for transpiration. The stem and leaf epidermis are provided with numerous stomata. questions.text: The translocation of organic solutes in sieve-tube members is supported by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cytoplasmic streaming is the movement of cytoplasm within a cell but is not the primary mechanism behind the long-distance transport of organic solutes in the phloem.
Root pressure and transpiration pull are involved in the movement of water (xylem transport) but are not directly responsible for the transport of organic solutes in the phloem.

P-proteins, also known as phloem proteins, are involved in sealing off damaged sieve-tube members to prevent the loss of sap. While they play a role in phloem physiology, they are not the primary drivers of organic solute translocation.

options.text: Mass flow involving a carrier and ATP
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. The movement of organic solutes in the phloem is driven by mass flow. Specialized cells called sieve-tube members transport organic solutes, such as sugars, through the phloem by utilizing carrier proteins and energy in the form of ATP. This mass flow mechanism allows for the efficient and long-distance transport of nutrients throughout the plant.

questions.explanation: According to the mass flow hypothesis, the transport of organic solutes takes place from source to sink, this transport also depends on metabolic energy. According to the cytoplasmic streaming hypothesis (which was given by de Vries, in 1885) the transport of organic solutes takes place by a combination of diffusion and cytoplasmic streaming. Cytoplasmic streaming carries organic solutes from one end to the other end of the sieve tube. P-proteins have a role as a defense against phloem-feeding insects and the sealing of damaged sieve tubes. So, the correct answer is 'Mass flow involving a carrier and ATP'. So correct option is C.

questions.text: Opening and closing of stomata is due to the:

options.text: Change in turgor pressure of guard cells
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. The opening and closing of stomata are primarily regulated by changes in the turgor pressure of the specialized cells called guard cells that surround the stomatal pore. When these guard cells gain water and increase in turgor pressure, they swell and cause the stomatal pore to open. Conversely, when they lose water and decrease turgor pressure, they shrink, leading to stomatal closure.

Gaseous exchange refers to the exchange of gases like oxygen and carbon dioxide, which occurs through stomata. However, gaseous exchange itself does not directly control the opening and closing of stomata.

Respiration is a metabolic process in plants, but it is not the direct mechanism responsible for the opening and closing of stomata. The control of stomatal aperture is primarily related to the turgor pressure of guard cells.

questions.explanation: It is turgor pressure in guard cells which determines the opening and closing of stomata. When the guard cells are swelled because of water absorption, it leads to opening of the stomatal pores. Once the water is lost, the guard cells become flaccid and the stomata closes. Also, during the daytime stomata generally remain open and during the night they remain closed. So correct option is B.

questions.text: Doctors use a stethoscope to hear the sounds produced during each cardiac cycle. The second sound is heard when:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: AV node receives a signal from SA node
options.explanation: The SA (sinoatrial) node is known as the natural pacemaker of the heart. It initiates electrical impulses that regulate the heart's rhythm. The AV (atrioventricular) node receives the electrical signal from the SA node and acts as a gateway, delaying the signal briefly before passing it to the ventricles. However, the second sound heard through a stethoscope does not occur due to the electrical activity of the heart.

The AV valves (tricuspid and mitral valves) are located between the atria and ventricles. They open during the cardiac cycle to allow blood to flow from the atria into the ventricles. The opening of the AV valves is associated with the first heart sound (S1), not the second sound heard through a stethoscope.

options.text: Ventricular walls vibrate due to gushing in of blood from atria
options.explanation: When the atria contract and push blood into the ventricles, it does create a small vibration in the ventricular walls. However, this vibration is not responsible for the second sound heard through a stethoscope.

options.text: Semilunar valves close down after the blood flows into vessels from ventricles
options.explanation: The semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonary valves) are located between the ventricles and the major blood vessels leaving the heart (aorta and pulmonary artery). They open during the cardiac cycle to allow blood to be ejected from the ventricles into the arteries. After the ventricles have contracted and ejected blood, the semilunar valves close to prevent backflow of blood into the ventricles. The closure of the semilunar valves produces the second heart sound (S2), also known as the "dub" sound, which is heard through a stethoscope.

questions.explanation: The correct option is d) Semilunar valves close down after the blood flows into vessels from ventricles. In summary, the second sound heard through a stethoscope occurs when the semilunar valves close down after the blood flows into vessels from the ventricles.

questions.text: Breakdown product of haemoglobin is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Bilirubin is a yellow-orange pigment that is produced during the breakdown of hemoglobin, the protein responsible for carrying oxygen in red blood cells. When red blood cells reach the end of their lifespan, they are broken down in the spleen and liver. The heme component of hemoglobin is converted to biliverdin, which is then converted to bilirubin. Bilirubin is released into the bloodstream and eventually processed by the liver and excreted in the bile.

Although iron is an essential component of hemoglobin, it is not considered a breakdown product of hemoglobin. Iron is recycled and reused in the body after the breakdown of hemoglobin, but it is not directly released as a breakdown product.

Biliverdin is an intermediate compound formed during the breakdown of heme in hemoglobin. Biliverdin is subsequently converted to bilirubin. Therefore, while biliverdin is involved in the breakdown process, it is not the final breakdown product of hemoglobin.
The correct option is not "Both A and B" because iron is not a breakdown product of hemoglobin. Only bilirubin (option A) is the breakdown product.

questions.text: When guard cells become turgid, transpiration?

options.explanation: When guard cells become turgid, it means that they are filled with water and have swelled up. This causes the stomata to open. When the stomata are open, it allows for the exchange of gases, including water vapor, between the plant and its environment. As a result, transpiration, which is the process of water loss through the stomata, increases.

If guard cells become turgid but there is no effect on transpiration, it would imply that the stomata do not open or do not open fully. In such a case, the exchange of gases, including water vapor, would be limited, and transpiration would remain unchanged. However, it's important to note that in most cases, turgid guard cells lead to the opening of stomata and increased transpiration.

When guard cells become turgid and open, it results in increased transpiration. Therefore, if the guard cells become flaccid or lose turgidity, the stomata close, reducing the exchange of gases and water vapor between the plant and its environment. As a result, transpiration decreases.

If guard cells become completely flaccid or lose turgidity to the extent that stomata close tightly, it can result in the complete cessation of transpiration. Closed stomata prevent the loss of water vapor from the plant, effectively stopping transpiration. However, it's important to note that complete cessation of transpiration is not a common occurrence and usually happens under specific conditions, such as extreme drought or during the night.

questions.explanation: Answer is “Increases”When sugar level rises i.e. solute concentration increases of water potential decreases- and the guard cells become turgid due to entry of water and they separate from one another, and stoma or pore opens.When guard cells become turgid, it leads to the opening of stomata, allowing for increased transpiration.

options.explanation: The phloem is generally found on the outer side of the xylem, forming a vascular bundle within the plant.

questions.text: Cell-mediated immune response is given by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cell-mediated immunity is an immune response that does not involve antibodies. Rather, cell- mediated immunity is the activation of phagocytes, antigen-specific cytotoxic T- lymphocytes, and the release of various cytokines in response to an antigen. Humoral immunity is also called antibody-mediated immunity. With assistance from helper T cells, B cells will differentiate into plasma B cells that can produce antibodies against a specific antigen. The humoral immune system deals with antigens from pathogens that are freely circulating, or outside the infected cells. Antibodies produced by the B cells will bind to antigens, neutralising them, or causing lysis (dissolution or destruction of cells by a lysin) or phagocytosis.

questions.text: In immunoglobulins / antibodies, Two light chains and two heavy chains are linked to each other by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Antibodies are proteins found in the blood and lymph that are secreted by the plasma cells arising from the B-lymphocytes. B-lymphocytes are white blood cells with a non-granular cytoplasm that are produced and matured in the bone marrow (primary lymphoid organ). B-lymphocytes migrate to secondary lymphoid organs like spleen, tonsils, lymph nodes, etc., when they mature and come in contact with trapped antigens. They undergo cell division and differentiation to form plasma cells which secrete antibodies. Each antibody is made up of two short light (L) peptide chains and two long heavy (H) peptide chains (H2L2) joined to each other by disulfide bonds. Disulfide bonds are covalent linkages between the sulphur-containing side chains of cysteine amino acids. The peptide chains are arranged in a Y shaped structure. Each chain in the antibody has both constant and variable regions. The amino acid sequence and conformation of the variable region keeps changing from one type of antibody to the other. The conformation and amino acid sequence of the constant region remains the same. The unique antigen binding site in each antibody that is compatible with specific antigens is composed of the variable regions of both the heavy and the light chains.

questions.text: The heartbeat cycle starts when electric impulses are generated from;
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: AV Node: This option is not the correct answer. The AV (atrioventricular) node is an important part of the electrical conduction system of the heart, but it is not the initial site where electric impulses are generated to start the heartbeat cycle.
SV Node: This option is not the correct answer. There is no specific node called the SV (Sinus Venosus) Node involved in the generation of electrical impulses for the heartbeat cycle.

SA Node: This option is the correct answer. The SA (sinoatrial) node, also known as the natural pacemaker of the heart, is the initial site where electric impulses are generated to start the heartbeat cycle. The SA node is located in the right atrium of the heart and sends electrical signals that cause the atria to contract.
PQ Node: This option is not the correct answer. There is no specific node called the PQ Node involved in the generation of electrical impulses for the heartbeat cycle.

questions.explanation: Antibodies are produced by specialized white blood cells called B lymphocytes (or B cells). B cells differentiate into plasma cells that produce antibody molecules closely modeled after the receptors of the precursor B cell. Once released into the blood and lymph, these antibody molecules bind to the target antigen (foreign substance) and initiate its neutralization or destruction. T lymphocytes are part of the immune system and develop from stem cells in the bone marrow. They help protect the body from infection and may help fight cancer by attacking foreign particles. Mucus is a thick, sticky substance that is produced by the lining of many organs, including the respiratory tract, digestive tract, and genitourinary tract. questions.text: A person got an infection, he became ill but then he survived. What do you think which type of immunity he would have developed?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This type of immunity occurs when a person's immune system responds to a natural infection, and the individual develops immunity as a result of their immune response after surviving the illness.
This type of immunity is acquired through vaccination, where a person's immune system is exposed to a weakened or inactivated pathogen to trigger an immune response.
Active immunity, in general, refers to the body's ability to produce an immune response against a specific pathogen, whether it occurs naturally or through vaccination.

Passive immunity involves receiving pre-formed antibodies (not produced by one's immune system) from an external source, such as maternal antibodies transferred to a newborn or through the administration of antibodies (like in certain treatments). This does not apply to someone who has survived an infection.

questions.text: T-lymphocytes recognize antigens and attack microorganisms or transplanted organs and tissues. This effect is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: T-lymphocytes play a central role in cell-mediated immunity, recognizing and attacking infected cells, cancer cells, and cells from transplanted organs.
The humoral immune response primarily involves B-lymphocytes producing antibodies to target antigens. It is not specific to T-lymphocytes' direct recognition and attack of antigens.

Active immunity is the result of an individual's immune system producing an immune response, often through vaccination or natural exposure to antigens. It encompasses both cell-mediated and humoral responses.

Passive immunity is the transfer of pre-formed antibodies, such as through maternal antibodies in breast milk or the administration of antibodies. It does not involve T-lymphocytes directly attacking antigens, as seen in a cell-mediated response.

questions.text: Which of the following facilitates opening of stomatal aperture?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Decrease in turgidity of guard cells
options.explanation: Decreased turgidity in guard cells leads to stomatal closure, not opening.

options.text: Radial orientation of cellulose microfibrils in the cell wall of guard cells
options.explanation: This doesn't facilitate stomatal opening. It's not related to the mechanism of stomatal regulation.

options.text: Longitudinal orientation of cellulose microfibrils in the cell wall of guard cells
options.explanation: This facilitates stomatal opening because it allows the guard cells to elongate and create an opening when the microfibrils align longitudinally.

options.text: Contraction of outer wall of guard cells
options.explanation: Contraction of the outer wall helps facilitate stomatal opening by causing the guard cells to bow outwards, creating an opening for gas exchange.

questions.explanation: When turgidity increases within the two guard cells flanking each stomatal aperture or pore, the thin outer wall bulge out and force the inner walls into a crescent shape. This results in the opening of stomata. The opening of stomata is also aided by the radial orientation of cellulose microfibrils in the cell wall of guard cells rather than longitudinal orientation. Decrease in turgidity closes the stomata. Radial orientation of cellulose microfibrils in the cell wall of guard cells causes opening of stomata. Contraction causes decrease in turgidity hence closing of stomata. So option B is correct.

questions.text: The lymph serves to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Return the interstitial fluid to the blood
options.explanation: Lymph (also called tissue fluid in the intercellular spaces) is the colourless liquid found within the lymphatic system. An important function of lymph is to return interstitial fluid back to the blood. Theinterstitial fluid is the filtered form of the blood without the cellular components and plasma proteins. It consists of water containing dissolved materials. It receives CO2, nitrogenous waste products, hormones and other synthetic substances from the tissue cells and enters the lymph capillaries to discharge them into blood.
